Threat.value: I always identify escape routes before I feel comfortable anywhere
I scan for exits as soon as I enter any room or building
Unusual silence makes me feel something dangerous is about to happen

Memory.value: The memory feels like it belongs to someone else rather than to me
The memory feels like it is happening right now rather than something that is over
There are parts of the event that I simply cannot remember no matter how hard I try

issues.item: Sexual Trauma Cues: Comments on sexual violence
issues.explanation: The indexed trauma is witnessed domestic violence in childhood. Sexual-violence comments imply a different trauma theme and are hard to reconcile unless sexual violence was part of the witnessed events, which is not indicated elsewhere in the profile.

For a childhood witnessed domestic violence trauma, ongoing real-world encounters with the offender are possible but not generally implied, especially in a 73-year-old profile. This item is questionable because the rest of the profile does not indicate current contact with the perpetrator or offender-focused appraisals/memories.

vignette:
Mariela is a 73-year-old divorced Latin American woman who has spent decades as a firefighter. She presents with persistent PTSD symptoms linked to repeated childhood exposure to domestic violence in the home. At the worst moments, she remembers hearing an adult male voice rise sharply in anger, the heavy smell of alcohol in the room, and, on one occasion, a burnt odor from the kitchen while violence was unfolding nearby. As a child, she did not intervene and could not get herself or others to safety. In the fire station and on call-outs, those same cues remain potent: a man’s tone changing abruptly, stale alcohol on someone’s breath, or the smell of something scorched can grip her attention immediately.

When Mariela speaks about the trauma, she does not describe it as a settled autobiographical memory. She says it can feel as if it happened to “some other little girl,” yet in the next moment she is pulled straight back into it, as though the shouting is happening now and the house is still dangerous. She becomes fixed on isolated fragments—especially the voice and the smell—while also saying there are stretches she cannot retrieve at all, no matter how much she tries to fill them in.

Her central stuck point is expressed in a spare, hardened way: nowhere is ever fully safe, and safety depends on being ready to get out immediately.

That stance is visible in her daily behaviour. She identifies escape routes before she can settle in any room. Entering a restaurant, briefing room, or unfamiliar apartment during a call, she automatically notes doors, windows, stairwells, and obstacles. She scans exits as soon as she comes through the entrance. Silence is also difficult for her; when a house or station suddenly goes quiet, she becomes tense and expects something dangerous to break through it.

The dominant maintaining cycle is tight and self-reinforcing. Sensory cues—especially related voices, alcohol smell, and burnt smell—rapidly evoke vivid fragments of the childhood scenes. Once this happens, her sense of current danger intensifies and she begins orienting urgently to exits and signs of threat. That sustained alertness leaves her even more primed to notice the next voice, odor, or unnatural silence, which in turn brings the memory closer again. On duty, this can narrow her focus. Colleagues may see her repeatedly checking egress points during routine building entry or becoming abruptly watchful in a quiet room, even when others do not register anything unusual.

satisfied_edges.explanation: Sensory cues are explicitly said to evoke vivid trauma fragments.
satisfied_edges.quote: Sensory cues—especially related voices, alcohol smell, and burnt smell—rapidly evoke vivid fragments of the childhood scenes.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The occurrence of memory fragments is explicitly followed by increased current danger in the same sentence with causal language.
satisfied_edges.quote: Once this happens, her sense of current danger intensifies and she begins orienting urgently to exits and signs of threat.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The memory is explicitly said to bring cues closer again, indicating memory contributes to renewed salience of triggers.
satisfied_edges.quote: That sustained alertness leaves her even more primed to notice the next voice, odor, or unnatural silence, which in turn brings the memory closer again.
